nativeRange.habitat: Euryhaline; found in coastal marine waters, estuaries, mangrove channels, tidal creeks, brackish lagoons, and fresh inland waters. Juveniles and sub-adults penetrate far inland into rivers, streams, and floodplains. Adults typically return to marine environments to spawn.

decorPreferences: robust filtration rated for heavy bioload
access to surface air essential (obligate air-breather)

diet.notes: Obligate piscivore; primarily consumes live fish, crustaceans, and large invertebrates in the wild. In public aquaria, may be trained onto large pieces of whole fish, whole prawns, and marine-based frozen foods over time. Will not readily accept dry or pelleted foods. Juveniles take smaller live and frozen prey (ghost shrimp, feeder fish, large bloodworms, earthworms). Strong feeding response triggered by movement.

goodWith: other large robust fish of similar size in very large public-aquarium settings (with caution)
avoidWith: any fish smaller than half its own body length (will be eaten)
conspecifics in small spaces (territorial and competitive)

imageCandidates.notes: FishBase species photo of Megalops cyprinoides; shows characteristic large scales, upturned mouth, and elongated dorsal fin ray.

summary: The Indo-Pacific Tarpon is a powerful, euryhaline piscivore reaching 1.5 m in the wild. An obligate air-breather that migrates across marine, brackish, and freshwater zones, it is strictly a public-aquarium species requiring 10,000 L+ tanks. Not suitable for home aquaria.
careNotes:
Megalops cyprinoides is the smaller of the two tarpon species — the Atlantic Tarpon (M. atlanticus) exceeds 2.5 m — but still attains 1.5 m total length and weights exceeding 15 kg in the wild, making it wholly unsuitable for home aquaria. It is a prized Indo-Pacific sport fish encountered throughout coastal East Africa, South Asia, Southeast Asia, and northern Australia.

The species is a classic euryhaline fish, moving freely between marine, brackish, and freshwater environments across its life history. Spawning occurs offshore in marine waters; leptocephalus larvae drift inshore and metamorphose in estuaries, after which juveniles and sub-adults penetrate rivers and streams, sometimes far inland. Adults re-migrate to marine habitats to reproduce.

A critical husbandry requirement is access to the water surface: like its Atlantic relative, M. cyprinoides possesses a physostomous, highly vascularised swim bladder that functions as an accessory air-breathing organ. The fish regularly gulps atmospheric air from the surface, and will suffocate if denied surface access even in well-oxygenated water. This is particularly important at elevated temperatures and in low-oxygen environments.

In public aquaria, juveniles may be maintained for display in large brackish or freshwater exhibits, but must be transitioned to proportionally larger systems as they grow. Water temperature should be maintained in the tropical range (22–30 °C); the species does not tolerate cold water. Robust mechanical and biological filtration is essential given the heavy bioload of a large active predator. The tarpon is an explosive jumper and exhibits strong startle responses — all exhibit structures must be fully enclosed with secure lids. Feeding on live or freshly dead whole fish and large crustaceans is standard; transition to frozen-thawed foods is possible with patience.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been achieved in aquarium settings. M. cyprinoides spawns in open marine waters; larvae are leptocephali that undergo a dramatic metamorphic shrinkage (as in eels and other elopomorphs) before recruiting to estuarine and freshwater habitats. The marine spawning requirement, large adult size, and complex migratory life history make captive reproduction impractical.

primary.name: FishBase — Megalops cyprinoides
primary.notes: Primary data source. Provided adult size (to 150 cm TL), temperature range, native range across Indo-Pacific, euryhaline status, habitat, taxonomy (Megalopidae, Elopiformes), diet (fish, crustaceans), and air-breathing biology notes.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish — Megalops cyprinoides
additional.notes: Provided husbandry notes on tank requirements, obligate air-breathing via swim bladder, euryhaline water parameter range (salinity tolerance, pH 6.5–8.5), feeding behaviour, and breeding biology including leptocephalus larval stage.

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — Indo-Pacific Tarpon / Oxeye Tarpon
additional.notes: Hobbyist-oriented overview covering captive care requirements, extreme adult size, public-aquarium suitability, jumping behaviour, and surface air-gulping requirement. Confirmed not suitable for home aquaria.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op — Brackish & Euryhaline Fish Overview
additional.notes: General brackish fish care context; Indo-Pacific Tarpon referenced as an example of a large euryhaline species requiring specialist public-aquarium setups. Supplementary only.

additional.name: Froese, R. & Pauly, D. (eds.) — FishBase species account cross-check
additional.notes: Lifespan, depth range (0–40 m), geographic distribution, and climate tolerance data cross-checked against FishBase ecological table entries.

fish.breedingNotes: Captive breeding not achieved. Requires open-ocean spawning; leptocephalus larval stage and large adult size preclude aquarium reproduction.

hobbyistAdvisory.reason: Reaches 1.5m+ in wild. Euryhaline (FW/brackish/marine). Requires very large public-aquarium tanks (10,000L+). Air-breather via swim bladder. Specialist single-fish setup; not viable for home aquaria.
